draft.introduction: Romans 8:14–17 is the passage where Paul draws the most radical conclusion from the doctrine of adoption. The argument builds in stages: those led by the Spirit are sons of God; they have received not a spirit of slavery but a spirit of adoption; the Spirit itself testifies that they are children of God; and if children, then heirs—heirs of God and co-heirs with Christ. The Greek synkleronomoi Christou (συγκληρονόμοι Χριστοῦ) makes the claim explicit: believers inherit alongside Christ, sharing the same inheritance from the same Father. The final clause adds a condition that the Fathers took seriously: “if indeed we suffer with him, so that we may also be glorified with him.” Theosis is not cheap. The path to shared glory runs through shared suffering. The Sinaiticus text preserves this passage from the fourth century, when the theology of co-inheritance with Christ was being articulated with increasing precision.

translation.text: For all who are led by the Spirit of God, these are sons of God. For you did not receive a spirit of slavery leading to fear again, but you received a spirit of adoption (huiothesia, υἱοθεσία), by which we cry, “Abba, Father!” The Spirit itself bears witness with our spirit that we are children of God, and if children, then heirs—heirs of God and co-heirs with Christ (synkleronomoi Christou, συγκληρονόμοι Χριστοῦ), if indeed we suffer with him, so that we may also be glorified with him.
translation.original_text: ὅσοι γὰρ πνεύματι θεοῦ ἄγονται · οὗτοι υἱοί εἰσιν θεοῦ · οὐ γὰρ ἐλάβετε πνεῦμα δουλείας πάλιν εἰς φόβον · ἀλλὰ ἐλάβετε πνεῦμα υἱοθεσίας · ἐν ὧ κράζομεν · Αββα ὁ πατήρ · αὐτὸ τὸ πνεῦμα συμμαρτυρεῖ τῷ πνεύματι ἡμῶν · ὅτι ἐσμὲν τέκνα θεοῦ · εἰ δὲ τέκνα · καὶ κληρονόμοι · κληρονόμοι μὲν θεοῦ · συγκληρονόμοι δὲ Χριστοῦ · εἴπερ συμπάσχομεν · ἵνα καὶ συνδοξασθῶμεν ·

translator_notes.note: The compound synkleronomoi (συγκληρονόμοι) is built from syn (with, together) and kleronomoi (heirs). Paul does not say believers inherit from God independently; he says they inherit jointly with Christ. The prefix syn- appears three times in rapid succession in verse 17: synkleronomoi (co-heirs), sympaschomen (we suffer with), syndoxasthomen (we are glorified with). This triple compounding makes the point unmistakable: the believer’s destiny is bound to Christ’s at every stage—in inheritance, in suffering, and in glorification. For the theosis tradition, co-glorification with Christ is participation in divine glory itself.

The phrase “spirit of adoption” (πνεῦμα υἱοθεσίας) parallels Galatians 4:6 but with a crucial difference. In Galatians, God sends the Spirit of his Son; here, believers receive a spirit of adoption. The two formulations complement each other: the Spirit that enables the cry “Abba” is both the Spirit of Christ and the spirit that constitutes adoption. This double identification—the Spirit of the Son is the spirit of adoption—means that to be adopted is to receive Christ’s own Spirit, to share in his own mode of relating to the Father.
